data.source: Online survey administered to the Italian population during the national Covid-19 lockdown
data.notes: Respondents came from five Italian macro-regions (1,751) and from abroad (126). Age 18–81 (M=37.67, SD=15.24); 1,283 females, 594 males.

method.description: Participants completed an online questionnaire in which they rated the funniness and aversiveness of various Covid-19-related and non-pandemic jokes. Personality traits (Coping Humor, Optimism) and psychological-distance measures (perceived risk of infection, spatial distance from the epidemic center, social distance measured as number of known Covid cases) as well as demographics were collected. Linear mixed-effects models were fit in R (lme4) to examine how joke type, personality, and distance variables predicted funniness and aversiveness ratings.
steps: Recruit Italian residents and a small number of respondents abroad to complete an online survey during lockdown.
Present participants with humor stimuli in four categories: Covid Verbal, Covid Meme, Covid Comic Strip, and Non-Covid Verbal jokes.
Collect 7-point scale ratings of Funniness and Aversiveness for each item.
Administer Coping Humor Scale and Life Orientation Test-Revised, and gather demographic information.
Measure psychological-distance variables: perceived risk (1–10 scale), spatial distance (km from Codogno outbreak epicenter), and social distance (number of acquaintances infected).
Fit hierarchical linear mixed-effects models with random effects for participants and items; incrementally add blocks of predictors (Demographics, Personality, Psychological Distance).
Compare model fit with likelihood-ratio tests and report fixed-effect estimates for key predictors.
method.models: Linear Mixed-Effects Models with random intercepts for participants and items; hierarchical model comparison via likelihood-ratio tests (AIC/BIC).
method.outcome_variable: 1. Funniness rating (7-point scale) 2. Aversiveness rating (7-point scale)
method.independent_variables: Humor type (Covid Verbal, Covid Meme, Covid Strip, Non-Covid baseline); Coping Humor Scale (CHS); Life Orientation Test-Revised (LOTR); perceived risk of infection; spatial distance from Codogno; social distance (known Covid cases).
method.control_variables: Demographics (age, gender, education); quadratic term for spatial distance (for funniness models).

results.summary: Covid-19 jokes were judged more aversive than non-pandemic jokes. Among Covid humor, Memes were considered the funniest, while Comic Strips were viewed as the most aversive. Individuals scoring high on the Coping Humor Scale and dispositional optimism found Covid humor funnier and less aversive. Higher perceived risk of contagion increased aversiveness, whereas greater spatial distance from the outbreak epicenter was associated with higher funniness ratings. The findings indicate that personality traits and psychological distance jointly shape emotional responses to Covid-19 humor.

numerical_results.outcome_name: Quadratic effect of Spatial Distance → Funniness
β (Perceived Risk → Aversiveness in Covid Strip)

metadata.original_paper_title: Funny but aversive: A large-scale survey on the emotional response to Covid-19 humor in the Italian population during the lockdown
